perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that the other party shall be entitled to equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, in the event of any breach of the provisions of this Agreement, in addition to all other remedies available to the other part at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that the other party may be entitled to remedies available at law.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance' and limiting the remedies, the contract is attempting to restrict the available remedies in case of a breach. This could violate the principle of providing adequate remedies, which includes equitable relief in cases where monetary damages are insufficient.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Contract Law - Adequate Remedies
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 359 states that specific performance or an injunction will not be ordered if damages would be adequate to protect the injured party. Removing the explicit mention of equitable relief and specific performance might be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent the availability of such remedies, even when they are appropriate under the law, which could potentially violate general principles of contract law regarding adequate remedies.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: remedy | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you remedy A remedy is a form of court enforcement of a legal right resulting from a successful civil lawsuit . Remedies are designed to address the harm suffered by a party due to the unlawful actions or omissions of another party. They ensure that justice is served by restoring the injured party to their original position or by providing relief appropriate to the circumstances. Remedies fall into three general categories: Damages - Monetary compensation for the plaintiff ’s losses , injury , and/or pain or restitutionary measures designed to restore the plaintiff’s status to what it was prior to the violation of his or her rights, Coercive remedies - Requiring a party to do or omit doing a specific act through injunctive relief or a court order of specific performance (a court mandates that the party fulfill contractual obligations. See Contracts ). Declaratory judgment - The court determines individual rights in a specific situation without awarding damages or ordering particular action. Because of their historical origins, monetary damages are often referred to as a legal remedy while coercive and declaratory remedies are termed equitable remedies. Plaintiffs can also receive provisional remedies when a court uses its discretionary power to prevent harm to the plaintiff while the plaintiff’s rights are still being determined. Such remedies include temporary injunctions , attachment , and garnishment . [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex accidents and injuries LIFE EVENTS tort damages THE LEGAL PROCESS courts wex definitions courts and procedure Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly allows for equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, whereas the changed text limits the remedies to those 'available at law,' omitting equitable remedies. This omission contradicts the principle of providing adequate remedies. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines remedy as 'a form of court enforcement of a legal right' and explains that remedies fall into three general categories, including coercive remedies like 'injunctive relief or a court order of specific performance.' By omitting the explicit mention of equitable relief, the contract attempts to restrict the availability of such remedies, creating a contradiction, as equitable remedies are indeed an important part of contract law.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that either party or any of its Representatives become legally compelled (by deposition, interrogatory, request for documents, subpoena, civil investigative demand or similar process) to disclose any of the Evaluation Material, such party shall provide the other party with prompt prior written notice of such requirement so that the other party may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that either party or any of its Representatives become legally compelled to disclose any of the Evaluation Material, such party may, at its discretion, notify the other party.
perturbation.explanation: This change makes the notification discretionary rather than mandatory. This could violate legal requirements for disclosure in some situations, like SEC regulations, where prompt and accurate disclosure is required.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 - Disclosure Requirements
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13(a) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 requires issuers of registered securities to file periodic reports with the SEC, disclosing material information. Making notification discretionary could hinder a company's ability to comply with these mandatory disclosure obligations, potentially violating federal securities laws. The MDI and Scientific Games are both publicly traded companies, meaning that they would be expected to comply with these laws.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.13a-11 - Current reports on Form 8-K (§ 249.308 of this chapter). | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13a-11 - Current reports on Form 8-K (§ 249.308 of this chapter). CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.13a-11 Current reports on Form 8-K (§ 249.308 of this chapter). (a) Except as provided in paragraph (b) of this section , every registrant subject to § 240.13a-1 shall file a current report on Form 8-K within the period specified in that form unless substantially the same information as that required by Form 8-K has been previously reported by the registrant . (b) This section shall not apply to foreign governments, foreign private issuers required to make reports on Form 6-K ( § 249.306 of this chapter) pursuant to § 240.13a-16, issuers of American Depositary Receipts for securities of any foreign issuer , or investment companies required to file reports pursuant to § 270.30a-1 of this chapter under the Investment Company Act of 1940 , except where such an investment company is required to file notice of a blackout period pursuant to § 245.104 of this chapter. (c) No failure to file a report on Form 8-K that is required solely pursuant to Item 1.01, 1.02, 1.05, 2.03, 2.04, 2.05, 2.06, 4.02(a), 5.02(e), or 6.03 of Form 8-K shall be deemed to be a violation of 15 U.S.C. 78j(b) and § 240.10b-5. perturbation.justification: The modified text changes a mandatory notification to a discretionary one, potentially conflicting with SEC regulations, as explained in the Law Explanation. Section 13(a) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 requires issuers to file periodic reports disclosing material information. By making notification discretionary, the changed text could hinder a company's compliance with these mandatory disclosure obligations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (15 USC 78m) describes the periodical reports required by issuers of registered securities. Scraped Law Snippet 2 (17 CFR § 240.13a-11) further details current report requirements on Form 8-K, meaning that the original 'shall' statement could provide a mechanism for compliance, while the modified text's 'may' statement weakens this protective mechanism.

perturbation.original_text: Each party understands and acknowledges that the other party is making no representation or warranty, express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Evaluation Material, and neither the disclosing party, nor any of its respective officers, directors, employees, stockholders, affiliates or agents will have any liability to the other party or any other person resulting from such other party's use of the Evaluation Material.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party understands and acknowledges that the other party is making no representation or warranty as to the Evaluation Material, and neither party will have any liability to the other party resulting from the use of the Evaluation Material.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness' and 'nor any of its respective officers, directors, employees, stockholders, affiliates or agents', this attempts to broaden the liability waiver. This could contradict laws that impose liability for fraudulent or intentionally misleading information, even in the absence of an express warranty.
perturbation.law_explanation: Even with a disclaimer of warranties, a party can be liable for fraudulent misrepresentation. The elements of fraudulent misrepresentation typically include a false statement of material fact, knowledge of its falsity, intent to induce reliance, justifiable reliance by the plaintiff, and resulting damages. This altered clause attempts to waive liability even if one party provides false information knowing it to be false, which contradicts fundamental principles of common law fraud and is generally unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: fraudulent misrepresentation | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you fraudulent misrepresentation Fraudulent misrepresentation is a tort claim , typically arising in the field of contract law, that occurs when a defendant makes a intentional or reckless misrepresentation of fact or opinion with the intention to coerce a party into action or inaction on the basis of that misrepresentation. To determine whether fraudulent misrepresentation occurred, the court will look for six factors: A representation was made The representation was false That when made, the defendant knew that the representation was false or that the defendant made the statement recklessly without knowledge of its truth That the fraudulent misrepresentation was made with the intention that the plaintiff rely on it That the plaintiff did rely on the fraudulent misrepresentation That the plaintiff suffered harm as a result of the fraudulent misrepresentation Like most claims under contract law, the standard remedy for fraudulent misrepresentation is damages . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords contract law contracts FRAUDULENT MISREPRESENTATION fraud Wex COMMERCE commercial activities LIFE EVENTS accidents & injuries (tort law) standards of tort liability tort damages business law commercial law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions courts and procedure Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the liability waiver, potentially conflicting with laws regarding fraudulent misrepresentation. The original text disclaims liability for the accuracy or completeness of the evaluation material by the disclosing party. The modified text omits the phrase 'express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness' and 'nor any of its respective officers, directors, employees, stockholders, affiliates or agents'. The 'Law Explanation' and 'Perturbation Explanation' both state that the changed text could allow a party to escape liability even if they provide false information, which is considered fraudulent. 'Scraped Law Snippet 1' states that fraudulent misrepresentation occurs when a defendant makes an intentional or reckless misrepresentation of fact with the intention to coerce a party into action. Thus, removing the explicit disclaimer on accuracy and completeness creates a contradiction by potentially shielding a party from liability for fraudulent misrepresentation, contradicting the law.
